title: CSR: Parasites & Prevention (Client-Facing)
description: What a CSR needs to know about parasites — not to diagnose, but to confidently answer common client questions, support prevention sales and scheduling, and recognize when to route concerns to the medical team.

modules.title: Module 1 — Parasite Basics for Client Conversations

lessons.title: Enough to Talk Confidently With Owners

blocks.content:
You don't need a technician's depth, but you'll field constant client questions about parasites, so a working foundation helps.

The big ones clients ask about:
• Fleas — itchy, visible, common; clients often see 'specks' (flea dirt).
• Ticks — clients worry about Lyme and finding ticks after walks.
• Heartworm — spread by mosquitoes, prevented with monthly products; clients ask why testing and prevention matter.
• Intestinal worms — roundworms, hookworms, tapeworms; clients notice 'worms' in stool or 'rice' near the tail.

Your role: answer general questions, reinforce why prevention matters, and route anything medical ('is my dog sick?') to the team. You're informed support, not a diagnostician.
When a client describes something that sounds medical ('he's losing weight and I saw worms'), capture it and route to the team rather than reassuring or interpreting.

blocks.front: What's the CSR's role regarding parasite questions?
blocks.back: Answer general informational questions and reinforce prevention — but route any 'is my pet sick' questions to the medical team.

blocks.question: A client asks, 'Why does my indoor cat need flea prevention? She never goes outside.' What's a good CSR response?

options.text: 'You're right, indoor cats don't need it.'
options.feedback: Inaccurate — fleas can come indoors on people, other pets, and pests; the medical team recommends prevention for good reason.

options.text: 'Fleas can still get inside on other pets or even on us, so the doctor recommends year-round prevention. I can tell you more or have the team explain the details.'
options.feedback: Correct — accurate general info, supports the recommendation, offers to route for detail.

options.text: Diagnose whether the cat currently has fleas

blocks.content:
A big part of the CSR role is supporting the clinic's prevention efforts and handling related logistics:

• Scheduling fecal tests, heartworm tests, and prevention refills.
• Explaining (at a general level) why year-round prevention is recommended.
• Helping clients understand product options the doctor recommended (not prescribing — supporting).

Zoonotic basics you should know: some parasites (like roundworms and hookworms) can affect people, especially children and those with weak immune systems. This is why the clinic emphasizes prevention, prompt stool cleanup, and hygiene. If a client asks detailed human-health questions, encourage them to talk to their physician and route veterinary questions to the team.
Zoonotic means it can spread between animals and people. You don't need details — just enough to take client concerns seriously and route them appropriately.

blocks.front: What does 'zoonotic' mean, and why does it matter to a CSR?
blocks.back: It means a parasite/disease can spread between animals and people — it's why prevention and hygiene matter, and why you take such concerns seriously.

blocks.prompt: A pregnant client mentions she's worried about catching something from the new puppy's worms. How do you handle it?

options.feedback: Some parasites are zoonotic and pregnancy is a sensitive situation — don't give medical reassurance.

options.text: Take her concern seriously, encourage her to talk with her physician, and route the puppy's parasite question to the medical team
options.feedback: Correct — validate, direct human-health questions to her doctor, and route the veterinary side to the team.

options.text: Give her detailed medical advice about toxoplasmosis
options.feedback: Outside your scope — route to the appropriate professionals.
